title: Why some friendships cannot survive your healing
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
You start to change. And the people around you feel it. 

 It's a strange thing, healing. You expect it to feel good. You expect to wake up one day lighte...
htmlContent:
You start to change. And the people around you feel it.

It's a strange thing, healing. You expect it to feel good. You expect to wake up one day lighter, freer, finally at peace. And sometimes that happens. But more often, healing feels like walking into a room where everyone else is still dancing to a song you can no longer hear. You're not judging them for dancing. You just can't join in anymore. And that distance, that quiet gap between your experience and theirs, starts to show.

I remember the first time I noticed it. I was sitting with a friend I'd known for over a decade. We were doing what we always did, complaining about our families, laughing at the same old jokes, drinking wine until the edges of our pain softened. But something was different. I felt my body tighten. I felt a quiet "no" rising in my chest. And I realized I didn't want to do this anymore. Not because I loved her less. But because I loved myself more.

Here's the thing. When you start healing your nervous system, you start listening to your body. And your body starts telling you things you don't want to hear. Like: this friendship hurts. Like: I'm not safe here. Like: I've been shrinking myself to fit into this relationship for years.

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body. It's not just a story in your head. It's a tightness in your shoulders, a knot in your stomach, a voice that goes quiet when someone else speaks. And when you start to release that trauma, your body starts to speak louder. It says no more. It says I deserve better. It says I will not pretend anymore.

And that is terrifying. Because the people who loved the old you, the one who numbed, who people-pleased, who stayed small, they don't always know what to do with the new you. They might even feel threatened. Not because you're attacking them. But because your healing is a mirror. And mirrors can be uncomfortable.

The contract you didn't know you signed

Every friendship has an unwritten contract. You don't sign it. You don't even know it exists. But it's there. It's built on shared habits, shared coping mechanisms, shared stories about who you are and what you deserve. Maybe your contract says: we will complain about our bodies together. Or: we will drink to feel okay. Or: we will never talk about what really happened in our childhoods. Or: we will rescue each other from our feelings instead of sitting with them.

When you start healing, you break that contract. You stop showing up to the same old roles. You stop playing the victim, the caretaker, the clown, the lost child. And the other person, the one who was counting on you to stay in your role, feels abandoned. They feel betrayed. And they might get angry. Or they might withdraw. Or they might try to pull you back into the old dance.

This is where it gets painful. Because you love them. You genuinely love them. But you also know that staying in that old contract will cost you your life. Not your physical life. But your soul. Your sense of self. Your ability to feel alive.

I had a friend who would call me every time she was in crisis. And I would drop everything. I would show up. I would fix it. That was our contract. She got rescued. I got to feel needed. But when I started healing my own trauma, I realized I was exhausted. I realized I was using her crises to avoid my own pain. And I realized I had to stop. So I did. I told her I loved her but I couldn't be her therapist anymore. She didn't speak to me for two years.

That hurt. It still hurts. But I know now that I didn't lose her. I lost the version of me that needed to be needed. And that was a loss worth grieving.

Gabor Mate, a physician and trauma specialist, often says that trauma is not what happens to you. It's what happens inside you as a result of what happens to you. And I think that applies to friendships too. The trauma of losing a friendship is not just the loss itself. It's the story you tell yourself about it. That you're unlovable. That you're too much. That you're breaking everything. But that story is not true. The truth is you're growing. And growth sometimes requires pruning.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work walks you through exactly what happens when your nervous system is stuck in survival mode, and how to slowly, gently, find your way back to safety. It helped me understand why I was so attached to friendships that were actually hurting me. And it gave me permission to let go.

The quiet grief of outgrowing people

No one talks about how lonely healing can be. We have this cultural image of the healer, the wise sage surrounded by adoring students, the person who has it all figured out. But real healing is messy. It's crying in your car after a coffee date. It's lying awake at night wondering if you're the problem. It's the quiet realization that some of the people you love most are not coming with you to the next place.

And that's not their fault. It's not your fault. It's just the way it is.

Think about it. If you've spent years numbing your feelings with food, or alcohol, or work, or relationships, and suddenly you stop, the people who were in those activities with you feel the shift. They might not say anything. But they feel it. And some of them will try to pull you back. Not out of malice. Out of fear. Because your presence was part of their safety system. And now that system is changing.

I've had friendships end over my healing journey in ways I never expected. One friend told me I was "too intense" because I wanted to talk about real things instead of small talk. Another said I was "judging her" because I stopped drinking. I wasn't judging her. I was just taking care of myself. But my self-care felt like criticism to her. That's the thing about healing. It exposes the unhealed places in others. And not everyone is ready to look.

Wild, right? You do the work. You go to therapy. You read the books. You learn to feel your feelings. And then you lose friends. It feels like a cruel joke. But it's actually a sign that you're on the right track. Because the people who can't handle your healing were never going to be able to support your growth. They were only comfortable with your stagnation.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz. IFS, or Internal Family Systems, has been a lifeline for me. It taught me that every part of me, even the part that clung to unhealthy friendships, was trying to protect me. And when I could thank that part instead of shaming it, I could finally let go with more compassion and less self-blame.

How to know when a friendship is costing you your healing

So how do you know when it's time to let go? There's no easy checklist. But there are some signs. Pay attention to your body. That's the first and most important thing. When you think about spending time with this person, does your stomach tighten? Do you feel heavy? Do you start making excuses to cancel? Do you feel drained after seeing them, not energized?

Notice what just happened in your body. Just reading those questions. Did something shift? That's your nervous system talking. And it's smarter than your brain sometimes.

Another sign is that the friendship is built on shared pain rather than shared growth. Some friendships survive because both people are in the same place, stuck in the same patterns, reinforcing each other's wounds. And that can feel very bonding. It can feel like the deepest connection in the world. But it's not connection. It's co-regulation through trauma. And it will keep you both stuck.

I'm not saying you have to cut off everyone who is struggling. That's not what healing is about. But if every conversation is about how broken you both are, if there's no room for hope, for change, for the possibility of something better, then you might be in a trauma bond, not a friendship.

And here's the hardest part. Sometimes you have to let go of people you genuinely love. People who have been with you through terrible times. People who are not bad people. But the relationship itself has become toxic. Not because anyone is malicious. But because the dynamic no longer serves your growth.

Stay with me here. This is where it gets . You don't have to make a dramatic exit. You don't have to announce that you're "cutting them off" or "setting a boundary" in a way that feels like a declaration of war. Sometimes you just quietly, gently, start to create more space. You say no to the coffee date. You don't answer the late-night call. You let the conversation drift toward lighter topics. You start investing more time in people who are also growing.

And some friendships will survive this shift. Some will deepen. Some will become more honest, more real, more grounded. But some won't. And that's okay. That's not a failure. That's just the natural cycle of life. People come into our lives for a reason, a season, or a lifetime. And we don't always get to choose which one they are.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) by Lindsay Gibson. It helped me understand why I was so drawn to friendships that mirrored my early family dynamics. Why I kept choosing people who couldn't see me, who needed me to be small, who punished me for growing. And it gave me a map for finding healthier relationships.

The open ending

I wish I could tell you that it gets easier. That eventually you find your tribe, your people, your soul family, and everything clicks into place. But that's not always how it works. What happens is you get more comfortable with your own company. You learn to trust your own nervous system. You become the person you were looking for in other people.

And that's not a consolation prize. That's the real work.

You might always carry a little grief for the friendships that couldn't survive your healing. I know I do. There are people I still miss, even though I know I couldn't stay. There are songs that make me think of them. There are old photos I can't delete. And that's okay. Grief is not a sign that you made the wrong choice. It's a sign that you loved.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach. It taught me that I could hold both the grief of losing a friendship and the joy of finding myself. That I didn't have to choose. That I could let the tears fall and still know I was on the right path.

So if you're reading this and you're in that painful place, the place where you're wondering if you're the problem, if you're too sensitive, if you're breaking everything, please hear me. You are not breaking. You are growing. And growth is not always gentle. It can feel like breaking. But it's not the same thing.

Some friendships cannot survive your healing. And that is not a tragedy. It is a truth. And truth, even when it hurts, is always a kind of liberation.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - Pete Walker's guide to understanding and recovering from C-PTSD
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - Richard Schwartz's introduction to Internal Family Systems therapy
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) - Lindsay Gibson's validation for those who grew up without emotional attunement
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - Tara Brach's compassionate approach to shame and self-judgment

As an Amazon Associate, I earn from qualifying purchases.
